root_text.sn52.7:1.2: Tatra kho āyasmā anuruddho bhikkhū āmantesi:
root_text.sn52.7:1.4: “Āvuso”ti kho te bhikkhū āyasmato anuruddhassa paccassosuṁ.
root_text.sn52.7:2.1: “Cattārome, āvuso, satipaṭṭhānā bhāvitā bahulīkatā taṇhākkhayāya saṁvattanti.
root_text.sn52.7:2.6: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ—
root_text.sn52.7:2.7: ime kho, āvuso, cattāro satipaṭṭhānā bhāvitā bahulīkatā taṇhākkhayāya saṁvattantī”ti.

translation_text.sn52.7:1.2: There Venerable Anuruddha addressed the mendicants:
translation_text.sn52.7:2.1: “Reverends, when these four kinds of mindfulness meditation are developed and cultivated they lead to the ending of craving.
translation_text.sn52.7:2.3: It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body …
translation_text.sn52.7:2.6: principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn52.7:2.7: When these four kinds of mindfulness meditation are developed and cultivated they lead to the ending of craving.”
